schools.summary: Theophylact: a lesson not to be slothful in teaching; Gregory Nazianzen: he consecrates many places by passing through them; Bede: like the eagle raising its young step by step.
The imperfect 'went about' (diodeuen) and distributive 'city and village'; 'afterward' (the word of Luke 1:3) marks Luke's care for sequence; an itinerant preacher on circuit (Henry).
Calvin: the Twelve attended as heralds to gather an audience, the teaching office not yet committed to them.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the fruits of repentance, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: The periphrastic past perfect — healed some time before, all with grounds of gratitude; the 'seven devils' a special malignity; no evidence Mary Magdalene was a prostitute, against tradition (Barnes, Clarke, Wesley).

debates.question: Was Mary Magdalene a former prostitute?

sides.position: No — the text gives not the least evidence; she was healed of demon-possession

schools.summary: Chuza steward to Herod Antipas, Joanna a convert of the upper class; Chuza perhaps the nobleman of John 4:46-53; cf. Manaen (Acts 13:1); the Idumean name (Lightfoot).
Calvin marks the pious zeal of wealthy matrons who supplied Christ's expenses from their own property and left their households to follow him.
Theophylact: parables make hearers attentive and veil the mystery from the unworthy; Origen: few walk the strait road.
Eusebius: the sower teaches two classes — those who fall through sloth and those who multiply the seed; Bede: the Sower is the Son of God going forth through the flesh.
The first great group of parables (Mark 4:1-34; Matthew 13:1-53), Luke giving only two; the parable perhaps spoken more than once (Ellicott).

outliers.note: The block here comments on the sinful woman of Luke 7:36-50, a catena offset, not this verse.

themes: the Sower the Son of God

schools.summary: 'His seed', 'trodden under foot', and 'of the heavens' peculiar to Luke (Matthew 13:3 for the full exposition); the Sower chiefly Christ (Gill).
Luke's 'upon the rock' and 'lacked moisture' (more precise than the others); the moisture the dew and rain of God's grace; the rock hearts hardened by sin (Gill).
The thorns grew with the seed and overpowered it (Ellicott); 'grew with it' (syn-) and 'choked' (Robertson); the thorny ground hearts filled with cares and pleasures (Gill).
Luke omits the thirty- and sixtyfold of Mark 4:8, dwelling on the hundredfold; 'he cried' (imperfect) the call to hear; the good ground honest hearts made so by the Spirit (Gill).

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the gift of the Spirit, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: The imperfect 'asked' (eager, repeated questioning); rightly 'what this parable meant'; Mark adds 'when he was alone' (Mark 4:10); asked when the multitude departed (Gill).

outliers.note: The note here concerns Abraham and Sarah, not this verse.

schools.summary: The mystery includes why many fail to enter through unfitness; the 'that' (hina) shares Matthew's causal 'because' (Matthew 13:13); the words from Isaiah 6:9; 'others' = those without (Gill).

outliers.note: A polemic against Manes on the blinding of unbelievers, only loosely related.

schools.summary: The article with subject and predicate makes them interchangeable — the word of God is the seed; the phrase frequent in Luke where Mark has 'the word' (Mark 4:14); taken in its widest sense (Ellicott).

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the subsistence of the Spirit, tangential to the verse.

themes: the seed is the word of God

schools.summary: Luke's 'the devil' (vs Satan / the wicked one) and the fuller aim 'lest they believe and be saved'; the devil snatches the seed before it sprouts; careless hearing (James 1:23-24).

themes: lest they believe and be saved

schools.summary: 'In time of trial' explained by the others' 'tribulation or persecution'; 'fall away' for 'are offended'; 'for a while believe' an ostensibly sincere start that drops out; the want of root an unrenewed heart (Hosea 6:4).
Luke's 'of life' (bios) and added 'pleasures' peculiar to him; the polished 'bring no fruit to perfection' (telesphorousin, only here); both word and hearer choked (1 Timothy 6:9-10).
'An honest and good heart' (kale kai agathe) peculiar to Luke, echoing the Greek ideal yet adding moral qualities; 'patience' = perseverance (Romans 2:7), a vigorous steadfastness.
Wesley: they keep it (not like the wayside), bear fruit (not like the thorny), with perseverance (not like the stony).
Bede/Eusebius: the mysteries veiled in parables are to be revealed, the lamp lit to give light; Augustine: the boldness of preaching, the candlestick the body given to God's service.
Chrysostom: the disciples, exposed to all men's view, must shine, for so great virtue cannot be hid.
Luke's portable lamp and general 'vessel' (vs 'the bushel'); the saying given in the Sermon (Matthew 5:15) and after the parable (Mark 4:21), here applied to the interpreting of the parable.
'That shall not be known' peculiar to Luke; a maxim often on Christ's lips (Matthew 10:26; Luke 12:2); 'what I teach in private ye shall teach publicly' (Clarke).
Wesley: strive not to conceal it at all, for you can conceal nothing long.

Luke's 'how ye hear' (the manner) vs Mark's 'what'; Lightfoot's rabbinic 'God's measure' — the full vessel receives more; 'that which he seemeth to have' = thinks he hath (1 Corinthians 10:12), unused knowledge lost.

outliers.note: A polemic against Manes, only loosely related to the verse.

schools.summary: Titus of Bostra: by grace, not nature, he made us his brethren; Chrysostom and Theophylact: no reproof of Mary but lifting her higher, some mocking his lowly birth.
Bede: the 'brethren' are kinsfolk, not children of Mary (against Helvidius) nor of Joseph by another wife.
Mark and Matthew place the visit before the parable; the brothers not yet openly hostile (John 2:12); Gill names Joses and Simon.

sides.position: Younger sons of Joseph and Mary

outliers.note: The block here comments on the parable of the sower, a catena offset, not this verse.

themes: could not reach him for the press

schools.summary: 'Was told' the common verb to bring word; given Mark's fuller report, perhaps a fact Luke learnt from Mark at Rome (Ellicott); textual variants in 'which said' (Gill).

'Mother and brothers to me are those who hear and do'; no one a child of God by human parentage (John 1:13); 'the word of God' (vs Mark's 'will') shows the phrase's fulness; hearing and doing together (Matthew 7:24).

outliers.note: The note here ('the nation was not gained') is misplaced.

schools.summary: Ambrose: no one passes this life without temptations — wake the Pilot who commands the winds; he who clings to Christ cannot perish; Bede recalls the risen Lord upbraiding unbelief.
Cyril: the Lord exposed the disciples to sea and winds that they too might know his benefits in their own persons.
'Launched forth' (anechthesan, put out to sea), a nautical term frequent in Luke-Acts; the crossing west to east (Matthew 8:23-24; Mark 4:35-41).

outliers.note: The block here comments on Christ's true kindred, a catena offset, not this verse.

schools.summary: 'He fell asleep' (aphypnosen, perhaps a medical word, the only mention of Christ's sleep); the wind-storm (lailaps) 'came down' through the Jordan gorge; 'they were filling'; the Jonah parallel (Jonah 1:4).

The repeated 'Master, Master' (epistata, Luke's word for Rabbi/Lord) shows their uneasiness; 'the raging of the water' (klydon) a violent surge; epistates Luke's classical word (Luke 5:5); the awaking (Psalm 44:23).
Better 'Who then is this?' and 'the water'; the question marks their astonishment; spoken to disciples who professed faith yet showed little (Gill).
Clarke: ye have power to believe yet do not exercise it; many look for more faith without using what they have.
'Gerasenes' correct here (as Mark), 'Gadarenes' in Matthew, cleared by Khersa (Gerasa) on the eastern bank near Gadara (Matthew 8:28; Mark 5:1); Titus of Bostra argues 'Gergesenes'.
Bede: Gerasa signifies the Gentile nations visited after the passion; Augustine reads the bound, broken-loose demoniac as a figure of the Gentiles under and beyond the law.

sides.position: Gerasenes here (Khersa on the eastern bank), Gadarenes in Matthew
Gergesenes, an ancient city by the lake

outliers.note: The block here comments on the stilling of the storm, a catena offset, not this verse.

schools.summary: Luke's physician's touch: he alone records the man 'ware no clothes' for a long time, a phenomenon of frenzied insanity; Mark implies it by 'clothed' at the cure; the demoniac homeless among the tombs.

outliers.note: The note here ('dumb, and deaf also') is misplaced.

schools.summary: 'Fell down' prostrate; 'the Most High God' a title common among heathen (Numbers 24:16), the demon speaking; the idiom 'what have I to do with thee?' (Mark 5:7); exact agreement with Mark.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the witnesses to Christ, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: The demon long seized the man by force, broke chains and fetters, drove him into the wilderness (Luke's plural 'deserts,' Robertson, Ellicott) — the destructive, isolating cruelty of possession no human restraint could hold; Christ makes haste to deliver (Wesley).

Christ asks the name not from ignorance but to reveal the magnitude of the possession to bystanders (Gill); 'Legion' (a Roman unit of thousands) discloses a host of demons in one man — magnifying both his misery and Christ's power; the Synoptics agree (Ellicott).

'The deep' = the abyss, the bottomless pit (Revelation 9), the demons' place of confinement, not the lake (Ellicott, Robertson); their plea confesses Christ's authority — they dread the doom he could pronounce and beg any fate rather than the abyss; Clarke illustrates from ancient demonology.

The demons beg to enter the swine (Luke's 'hikanos' for 'many,' Robertson) not for relief (they soon destroy the herd, Wesley) but from destructiveness; Christ's 'he suffered them' a deliberate act of sovereignty serving his purposes — making the possession visible and showing the demons' murderous intent.

The demons transfer to the swine, which rush down the cliff and drown (vivid verbs, Robertson); Clarke firmly rejects the rationalist misreading (the maniac merely driving the swine) — the text states a real transfer; the destruction confirms the deliverance and the demons' malignity.

debates.question: Was this a real transfer of demons, or merely the maniac driving the swine?

sides.position: A real transfer of the demons into the swine, as the text plainly states
A rationalist reading: the madman ran after and drove the swine (firmly rejected)

themes: the devils entered into the swine
ran violently down a steep place

schools.summary: The swineherds flee and report the deliverance and the herd's loss through city and farms — the news spreading by their alarmed telling (Luke's neat Greek, Robertson); it brings the multitude out (v.35) and publishes Christ's power.

The delivered man 'clothed, and in his right mind, sitting at the feet of Jesus' — the picture of restored humanity and the disciple's posture (peculiar to Luke, recalling Mary, Ellicott); the crowd's fear not holy awe but a dread leading to rejection (v.37).

themes: sitting at the feet of Jesus
clothed and in his right mind

schools.summary: The eyewitnesses tell how the demoniac was 'made whole' (sozo, the word for salvation, Robertson) — relating the manner, not just the fact (Ellicott); the confirming testimony establishing a public, well-attested miracle, yet not producing faith in the fearful crowd (v.37).

themes: they which saw it told them
by what means he was healed

schools.summary: The Gadarenes, gripped by great fear (Robertson, Ellicott), beseech Christ to depart — preferring their lost swine to the Saviour; a fear of unbelief and worldliness, not worshipping awe; Christ departs, leaving them to their choice (contrast the Galileans, v.40, Henry).

Calvin: the delivered man longs to stay (contrast the Gadarenes who flee) — the difference between knowing God's wrath (which terrifies) and his grace (which draws gently); Christ refuses not in rejection but to make him a witness to his Gentile countrymen.
The man kept begging to remain (Robertson); Christ sends him away as a commission, not a refusal — the first missionary to the Decapolis.

themes: that he might be with him

schools.summary: Calvin: Christ bids him publish 'how great things God hath done,' instructing an ignorant people gradually toward his divinity; the bodily deliverance figures the spiritual — every soul held by Satan, naked and torn, until Christ restores it; gratitude makes us heralds.
The man, told to publish God's work, publishes Jesus's — rightly identifying Christ with God; the first witness to the Decapolis, telling his story with power (Robertson).

themes: shew how great things God hath done

schools.summary: The intertwined miracles read allegorically: Jairus/the Law (Moses), his dying 12-year daughter/the Synagogue, the 12-year bleeding woman/the Gentile Church — while the Synagogue flourished the Church was weak, until the Gentiles seized the health prepared for Israel (Ambrose, Bede).
Henry: the Galileans gladly receive Christ, in contrast to the Gadarenes — if some reject the favours Christ offers, others accept them.
Christ undertakes Jairus's request knowing he will do more than asked — raise the dead and implant the hope of resurrection (Chrysostom); the throng a sign of the real incarnation (Cyril).

themes: the allegory of Synagogue and Church

schools.summary: Henry: Jairus, though a ruler, falls at Christ's feet, humble and importunate; Christ complies — strong faith applauded, weak faith not rejected; the presence of Christ desirable in every house of sickness.
His rank closes the mouths of the Jews (Titus Bostrensis); yet his weak faith thinks Christ must come in person, unlike the centurion who trusted a word (Chrysostom).

The only daughter (monogenes, as of the widow's son and Christ, Robertson), about twelve, lay dying — Luke names the age with a physician's precision at the start (Ellicott); the crowd 'thronged' (the verb of thorns choking grain).
The dying 12-year daughter allegorically the Synagogue, nobly raised by the prophets but perishing at maturity until Christ helps it (Bede); the throng the carnal people who press but do not believe.

A chronic 12-year issue of blood, all her living spent on physicians who could not heal — Luke the physician treats the doctors gently (omitting Mark's 'grew worse,' Ellicott, Robertson); a case beyond all human help.
The bleeding woman allegorically the Gentile Church, defiled by idolatry, who wasted all on false remedies (false teachers, philosophers, demons) yet remained incurable until Christ (Bede).

themes: an issue of blood twelve years

schools.summary: The woman, ashamed, touches only the tassel of Christ's garment from behind, and the issue stops at once (the effective aorist, Robertson) — the reward of faith, not magic in the cloth.
The touch the act of faith — she touched Christ with her mind before her body (Theophylact); her earnest faith, not the garment, saved her (Chrysostom, Ambrose).

themes: touched the border of his garment

schools.summary: Calvin: Christ knowingly cured her but asks 'Who touched me?' that she may publicly confess — lest the miracle rest on his testimony alone; her trembling confession gives it greater weight.

Christ asks not from ignorance but to bring the secret healing and faith to light (Gill); the disciples see only the crowd pressing and crushing (Robertson), missing the distinction between the press and the touch of faith.

Clarke: the divine virtue did not flow automatically to all who touched Christ but to this woman alone, because she came in faith — faith alone attracts the energetic influence of God; more faith would bring more miracles.
Christ distinguishes the one believing touch from the crowd's accidental press (Gill); 'virtue gone out' (Luke's medical term, Ellicott) a real communication of healing power answering faith.

themes: virtue is gone out of me

schools.summary: The woman, unable to remain hidden, comes trembling and declares before all why she touched him and how she was healed — facing the widest publicity for her secret cure (Robertson); the hidden faith brought to open testimony.
Chrysostom: Christ draws out the confession to remove her fear, reprove her concealment, and make the miracle known for others' sake.
Christ tenderly calls her 'Daughter,' confirms 'thy faith hath made thee whole,' and dismisses her in peace (as the sinful woman, Luke 7:50) — far from rebuking her secret touch, he welcomes her.
'Daughter' grants the grace of adoption for faith (Titus Bostrensis); her soul healed by faith before her body (Origen).

themes: thy faith hath made thee whole

schools.summary: The delay (the woman's cure) was providential, allowing the greater miracle of resurrection to be made public (Chrysostom); 'trouble not the Master' the voice of unbelief, thinking resurrection impossible (Ambrose).

Augustine harmonizes Matthew (daughter reported dead) with Luke/Mark (dying, then dead) — the accounts agree Christ would raise the dead; Bede reads the message allegorically of those who think the dead Synagogue cannot be restored.

debates.question: How is Matthew's report (daughter already dead) reconciled with Luke/Mark (dying, then dead)?

sides.position: Matthew abridges for brevity, reporting the outcome Christ was asked to effect; the accounts agree

schools.summary: Christ bids 'fear not, believe only' — Luke stressing the immediate act of faith (Ellicott) and adding the promise 'she shall be made whole' (Robertson); the call to faith precisely where human hope fails.
Christ strengthens rather than blames Jairus's faith (Augustine); he requires faith not from need but that his gifts be received and held (Athanasius).

themes: faith at the point of death

schools.summary: Christ admits only the inner three (Peter, James, John, named by Luke and Mark, Ellicott) and the parents to the raising.

The restriction teaches humility — doing nothing for show (Theophylact) — and secures credible witnesses: the chief disciples able to conceal it, and the parents lest the resurrection be thought false (Chrysostom).

themes: suffered no man to go in

schools.summary: Calvin: the lamentation (hired mourners, flutes) confirms the real death (so the resurrection be more believed) and exposes the disordered, near-pagan grief-customs of corrupt Judea.
'Not dead but sleepeth' — to Christ who would raise her, the separation is but a short sleep (Wesley); he would awaken her as from slumber (Chrysostom).

themes: she is not dead but sleepeth

schools.summary: The mourners, knowing she was truly dead (Robertson), laugh Christ to scorn — their scornful certainty the ironic proof that the raising is a true resurrection; the swift turn from weeping to mocking exposes the unbelief of the crowd (whoever believes not, laughs, Ambrose).

Christ puts out the mocking crowd (pompous funeral customs alien to his religion, Clarke), takes the girl's hand, and calls 'Maid, arise' — the touch and voice restoring life (Robertson); whether truly dead or in a trance 'matters little,' his power restored her.
Chrysostom: Christ restored the very soul she had breathed forth, not another; the call made that all might see she rose at his word.
'Her spirit came again' (Luke's precise physician's phrase, Ellicott) attests the soul's immortality, separate existence, and reunion with the body (Gill).

Clarke: raised by miracle but not preserved by miracle — God works by ordinary means (nature his instrument) and makes us workers with him; he provides salvation but neither repents nor believes for us; the feeding confirms the reality of the resurrection (Chrysostom).

Christ charges the astonished parents to silence — avoiding vain-glory (he gives freely, seeking no glory, Greek Exposition) and prudent regarding the time and the envy of the Jews, not revealing himself prematurely (Chrysostom).
The parents astonished at the resurrection; Christ's charge to tell no man (Gill, Barnes).

themes: tell no man what was done
